source_paper_title: Lipschitz Stability of Least-Squares Problems Regularized by Functions with C2-Cone Reducible Conjugates
context:
Section 5 (Conclusion), page 20 (PDF pages 19–20 in the provided parse)

One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future is the computation of the Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping when Lipschitz stability occurs. In theory, this Lipschitz modulus may be obtained by calculating the coderivative of the solution mapping (30); see, e.g., [20, Theorem 4.10] and [27, Theorem 9.39]. However, this approach requires some involved computations of second-order structures of the regularizer \(g\), which can be particularly challenging when \(g\) does not have a polyhedral structure.
title: Compute the local Lipschitz modulus of the regularized least-squares solution mapping without using second-order information of the regularizer
latex:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces, let \(\mathcal L(X,Y)\) denote the space of linear maps \(A:X\to Y\), and let \(g:X\to\mathbb R\cup\{+\infty\}\) be closed, proper, and convex. For parameters \((A,b,\mu)\in \mathcal L(X,Y)\times Y\times \mathbb R_{++}\), define the (possibly set-valued) solution mapping
\[
S(A,b,\mu):=\operatorname*{argmin}_{x\in X}\left\{\frac{1}{2\mu}\|Ax-b\|^2+g(x)\right\}.
\]
Fix \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\in \mathcal L(X,Y)\times Y\times \mathbb R_{++}\) and suppose \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz continuous at \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\), with \(\bar x:=S(\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\). Define the local Lipschitz modulus of \(S\) at \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) by
\[
\operatorname{lip} S(\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)
:=\inf\Big\{L\ge 0:\exists\ \delta>0\ \text{s.t.}\ \|S(p)-S(q)\|\le L\|p-q\|\ \forall p,q\in B_\delta(\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\Big\},
\]
where \(p=(A,b,\mu)\), \(q=(A',b',\mu')\), \(\|p-q\|\) is any fixed norm on \(\mathcal L(X,Y)\times Y\times \mathbb R\), and \(B_\delta(\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) denotes the closed ball of radius \(\delta\) centered at \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) under that norm.

Problem: Derive an explicit computable formula or tight computable bounds for \(\operatorname{lip} S(\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) that avoids computing second-order variational objects of \(g\) (e.g., coderivatives/second-order subdifferentials of \(\partial g\)), and instead uses only first-order information about \(g\) (e.g., \(\partial g\), \(\partial g^*\), and their associated parallel subspaces) together with the data \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu,\bar x)\).

open_problem_quote: One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future is the computation of the Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping when Lipschitz stability occurs. In theory, this Lipschitz modulus may be obtained by calculating the coderivative of the solution mapping (30); see, e.g., [20, Theorem 4.10] and [27, Theorem 9.39]. However, this approach requires some involved computations of second-order structures of the regularizer \(g\), which can be particularly challenging when \(g\) does not have a polyhedral structure.
